title: Cauda Equina Syndrome
chief_complaint: I have severe low back pain going down both legs, my legs are weak, I can't feel my bottom area when I wipe, and I can't control my bladder - I'm leaking urine and I don't even feel it. This started 24 hours ago and is getting worse.

physical_exam.Neurologic: Bilateral leg weakness (L5-S1 myotomes: ankle dorsiflexion 3/5, plantarflexion 3/5). Absent ankle reflexes bilaterally. Saddle anesthesia (S2-S5 dermatomes - perineum, inner thighs, buttocks). Loss of anal sphincter tone on digital rectal exam (absent anal wink).
physical_exam.Straight_leg: Positive straight leg raise at 30° bilaterally.

hidden_data.mri_lumbar: Large central disc herniation at L4-L5 with severe canal stenosis. Compression of cauda equina nerve roots. No conus medullaris involvement (lesion below L1-L2).
hidden_data.ct_lumbar: Large disc herniation L4-L5. Canal compromise >75%.

correct_diagnosis: Cauda Equina Syndrome from Massive L4-L5 Central Disc Herniation
key_learning_points: Cauda equina syndrome = compression of lumbosacral nerve roots below conus medullaris (below L1-L2). SURGICAL EMERGENCY - requires decompression within 24-48h to prevent permanent paralysis, incontinence, and sexual dysfunction

RED FLAGS: Bilateral sciatica, Saddle anesthesia (pathognomonic), Bladder/bowel dysfunction (urinary retention - most common, overflow incontinence, fecal incontinence), Loss of anal sphincter tone, Bilateral leg weakness/reflex loss. Ask about these symptoms in every patient with severe back pain!
Causes: Massive central disc herniation (most common - L4-L5 or L5-S1), Trauma (burst fracture), Tumor, Epidural abscess/hematoma, Spinal stenosis
Diagnosis: CLINICAL. MRI lumbar spine EMERGENTLY (do NOT delay for XR or CT). If MRI contraindicated: CT myelogram. Post-void residual >200mL suggests bladder dysfunction

Treatment: EMERGENCY SURGICAL DECOMPRESSION (laminectomy + discectomy). Timing: Within 24-48h of symptom onset. Delayed surgery = worse neurologic recovery. Bladder function recovery: 70% if surgery <24h, 30% if >48h. Pre-operative: Foley catheter, IV steroids controversial (may reduce edema)

Medicolegal emergency: One of the most common causes of malpractice lawsuits in emergency medicine and orthopedics. MISSED diagnosis = permanent disability. Have LOW threshold for MRI in patients with red flag symptoms
